scenario_name: Scenario 20 — High-Stakes Implementation Under Incomplete Spec
justification: AC1-AC5 correctly implemented with floor rounding via Math.floor(x*100)/100. Tests cover AC3 exact value ($66.50), floor rounding with repeating decimals, and zero case. However, unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) are noted in assumptions but not documented in the deviations section (which is empty), and confidence does not differentiate AC1-AC5 as HIGH vs edge cases as LOWER — it uses a single numeric level (97) rather than marking edge case behaviors at lower confidence. The rubric requires 'Confidence section: AC1-AC5 HIGH, edge case behaviors LOWER with notes' and 'Unspecified edge cases documented as assumptions in deviations'.

observations.text: Floor rounding correctly implemented with Math.floor(x*100)/100, not round-half-up
AC3 exact value test ($66.50) included and passing
Floor rounding explicitly tested with repeating-decimal cases that would diverge under round-half-up ($66.66 not $66.67, $3.33 not $3.34)
AC5 zero case covered with two test variants
Floating-point precision concern noted in low_confidence_areas
Edge case assumptions documented in confidence.assumptions section
Deviations array is empty — rubric requires unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) documented there as assumptions
Confidence uses single numeric score (97) rather than marking AC1-AC5 HIGH and edge case behaviors LOWER with explicit notes as rubric requires
No escalation raised for unspecified edge cases — rubric expects either conservative validation with escalation note OR explicit escalation

trials.justification: AC1-AC3 correctly implemented with canonical $66.50 example passing. AC4 uses Math.floor(rawPayout * 100) / 100 (correct floor rounding, not round-half-up) with four distinct floor tests. AC5 returns 0 for zero input. Unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) escalated to Bird with clear question rather than blocking implementation. Confidence is high on AC1-AC5 and lower on floating-point edge cases. Assumptions documented in confidence section. Financial precision concern noted regarding IEEE 754 limits.

observations.text: Floor rounding correctly implemented via Math.floor(x*100)/100 with multiple edge case tests including 1/3 and 2/3 values
Escalation for unspecified edge cases is well-structured — identifies exact gaps, doesn't block core work, and routes to Bird with actionable question
12/12 tests passing including AC3 exact value, floor rounding, and zero case
Deviations array is empty — the assumptions about input validation behavior could have been documented there rather than only in escalations and confidence.assumptions
Floating-point concern noted in low_confidence_areas with IEEE 754 context
Confidence calibration is correct: high on specified ACs, lower on unspecified edge cases

trials.justification: AC1-AC3 implemented exactly as specified with correct formula. AC4 uses Math.floor on cent-integer representation (Math.floor(x * 100) / 100), not round-half-up. AC5 zero case handled. Unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) documented in deviations with clear justification. Confidence section marks AC1-AC5 as high confidence, with floating-point precision as a low-confidence area. Tests cover AC3 exact value (66.5), multiple floor rounding cases including a 0.5-cent edge that distinguishes floor from round-half-up, zero case, and boundary cases. Financial precision concern explicitly noted in low_confidence_areas.

observations.text: Floor rounding implemented correctly with Math.floor on cent-integer representation, with a 0.5-cent test that conclusively distinguishes floor from round-half-up
Thoughtful deviation rationale — explains why silent clamping would mask upstream bugs in payment systems rather than just omitting validation
14 passing tests covering all ACs plus boundary cases (zero share, 100% fee, sub-dollar, large amounts)
Floating-point precision concern explicitly called out as low-confidence area

Confidence level 97 is reasonable but edge cases confidence could be more explicitly separated — the structured format groups AC1-AC5 as high but doesn't assign a distinct lower numeric confidence to the unspecified edge case decisions
No explicit escalation for unspecified edge cases — chose to document assumptions instead, which is acceptable but a brief escalation note would have been ideal per rubric
